reasoning: Confirmed. The sink at line 345 (`do_action('customize_save_' . $key, $wp_customize)`) is reachable with attacker-controlled key names. Control-flow: (1) outer gate requires `edit_theme_options` (line 58); (2) inner gate `can_import()` requires `install_plugins` (line 44/246) — correctly stated in the finding; (3) nonce at line 251 is satisfied trivially by any authenticated user who already has `install_plugins`; (4) deserialization at line 294 uses `allowed_classes => false` which prevents object injection but leaves the array structure fully attacker-controlled; (5) the template-name check at line 308 is bypassable — the attacker knows or can discover the active theme name via export; (6) `$key` flows from `$data['mods']` array keys directly into `do_action()` with zero sanitization — no `sanitize_key()`, no allowlist, no stripping. The action receives only `$wp_customize` as its argument so the attacker cannot inject arbitrary data through the hook argument; the impact is limited to triggering side effects in any third-party callback registered on the `customize_save_*` hook namespace. Severity medium is appropriate: not RCE, not direct data write, but unintended dispatch of registered hooks with real-world consequences depending on installed theme/plugin ecosystem.
